content.text: Azure CLI output format and query options determine how results are shaped before reaching your terminal or script. The -o flag controls the serialisation format (table, json, tsv, yaml). The --query flag applies a JMESPath expression that filters, projects, and transforms the JSON before it is serialised. Together they eliminate most post-processing pipelines — you get exactly the data you need in the form you need it.

attrs.alt: Azure CLI output formats side by side: -o table (human-readable columns), -o json (full object), -o tsv (shell-friendly tab-separated), -o yaml (config-like). JMESPath query examples shown below.

content.text: Full data; piping to jq or Python
JSON with colour highlighting (same data as json)
Suppress output; only check exit code

# table — columns chosen by CLI, truncated for wide values:
az vm list -o table
# NAME        RESOURCE GROUP    LOCATION    ZONES
# vm-web-01   rg-prod           eastus
# vm-app-01   rg-prod           eastus

# json — full object graph:
az vm list -o json | head -20

# tsv — tab-separated, no header row:
az vm list --query "[].name" -o tsv
# vm-web-01
# vm-app-01

# yaml — indented key:value:
az vm show --name vm-web-01 --resource-group rg-prod -o yaml | head -15

# none — exit code only (0 = success, non-zero = failure):
az group show --name rg-prod -o none && echo "exists" || echo "not found"

JMESPath is the query language used by --query. It operates on the JSON response before output formatting. The key operations are: field selection, array indexing, filtering, projection, and aggregation.

# Select a top-level field from an object:
az vm show --name vm-web-01 --resource-group rg-prod --query "name" -o tsv
# vm-web-01

# Select a nested field:
az vm show --name vm-web-01 --resource-group rg-prod --query "hardwareProfile.vmSize" -o tsv
# Standard_D2s_v3

# Select a deeply nested field:
az vm show --name vm-web-01 --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "storageProfile.osDisk.diskSizeGB" -o tsv
# 30

# Select from an array element:
az vm show --name vm-web-01 --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "networkProfile.networkInterfaces[0].id" -o tsv

# Select one field from every element in a list:
az vm list --resource-group rg-prod --query "[].name" -o tsv

# Select multiple fields from each element (projection):
az vm list --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[].{Name:name, Location:location}" -o table

# Rename the columns in the projection:
az vm list --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[].{VM:name, RG:resourceGroup, Region:location, Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize}" \
  -o table

# Get the first element:
az vm list --query "[0].name" -o tsv

# Get the last element:
az vm list --query "[-1].name" -o tsv

# Slice a list (elements 0-2):
az vm list --query "[0:3].name" -o tsv

# Filter by exact field value (use backticks for string literals):
az vm list --query "[?location==\`eastus\`].name" -o tsv

# Filter by resource group:
az resource list \
  --query "[?resourceGroup==\`rg-prod\`].{Name:name, Type:type}" \
  -o table

# Filter by a nested field value:
az vm list \
  --query "[?hardwareProfile.vmSize==\`Standard_D2s_v3\`].name" \
  -o tsv

# Filter using contains() (substring match):
az vm list \
  --query "[?contains(name, \`web\`)].name" \
  -o tsv

# Filter using starts_with():
az vm list \
  --query "[?starts_with(name, \`vm-web\`)].name" \
  -o tsv

# Combine filters with && (AND):
az vm list \
  --query "[?location==\`eastus\` && resourceGroup==\`rg-prod\`].name" \
  -o tsv

# Count items in a list:
az vm list --query "length(@)" -o tsv

# Count filtered items:
az vm list --query "length([?location==\`eastus\`])" -o tsv

# Sort a list by a field:
az vm list --query "sort_by(@, &name)[].name" -o tsv
# sort_by requires & prefix on the sort key (it's a JMESPath expression reference)

# Sort descending (reverse sort_by result):
az vm list --query "reverse(sort_by(@, &name))[].name" -o tsv

# Get unique values (no built-in, use shell):
az vm list --query "[].location" -o tsv | sort -u

# Sum numeric values with Python:
az vm list --query "[].{Name:name}" -o tsv | wc -l
These patterns cover the most common CLI scripting tasks where --query eliminates the need for jq, Python, or awk post-processing.

# Capture a VM's resource ID for use in subsequent commands:
VM_ID=$(az vm show \
  --name vm-web-01 \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query id \
  -o tsv)

# Use the ID in another command:
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --name diag-vm-web-01 \
  --resource "$VM_ID" \
  --workspace /subscriptions/.../workspaces/law-prod \
  --metrics '[{"category":"AllMetrics","enabled":true}]'
Capture Multiple Fields into Shell Variables

# Read multiple fields in a single CLI call (tsv projection):
read -r NAME LOCATION SIZE <<< $(az vm show \
  --name vm-web-01 \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[name, location, hardwareProfile.vmSize]" \
  -o tsv)

echo "Name: $NAME"
echo "Location: $LOCATION"
echo "Size: $SIZE"

# Loop over all VMs in a resource group and print their status:
az vm list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[].name" \
  -o tsv | while read -r VM; do
    STATE=$(az vm get-instance-view \
      --name "$VM" \
      --resource-group rg-prod \
      --query "instanceView.statuses[1].displayStatus" \
      -o tsv)
    echo "$VM: $STATE"
done

# Loop over all storage accounts missing a required tag:
az resource list \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts \
  --query "[?tags.Environment==null].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup}" \
  -o tsv | while read -r NAME RG; do
    echo "Adding Environment tag to $NAME in $RG"
    az resource tag \
      --resource-group "$RG" \
      --name "$NAME" \
      --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts \
      --tags Environment=Production \
      --is-incremental
done
Build a Report with Table Output

# Generate a cross-subscription inventory report:
SUBS=$(az account list --query "[?state=='Enabled'].id" -o tsv)

echo "SUBSCRIPTION | NAME | RG | TYPE | LOCATION"
echo "---|---|---|---|---"

for SUB in $SUBS; do
  az resource list \
    --subscription "$SUB" \
    --query "[].{Sub:\`$SUB\`, Name:name, RG:resourceGroup, Type:type, Location:location}" \
    -o tsv
done | column -t -s $'\t'
For complex transformations that JMESPath cannot express (grouping, joins, custom aggregation), pipe CLI output to jq. Use --query to reduce the payload first, then jq for the complex part.

# Count VMs per location using jq:
az vm list --query "[].location" -o json | jq 'group_by(.) | map({location: .[0], count: length})'

# Find the largest VM size in each resource group:
az vm list --query "[].{RG:resourceGroup, Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize}" -o json | \
  jq 'group_by(.RG) | map({rg: .[0].RG, sizes: [.[].Size] | unique})'

# Extract all IPs from network interfaces:
az network nic list --resource-group rg-prod -o json | \
  jq '[.[].ipConfigurations[].privateIPAddress]'

# Filter out null values:
az vm list --query "[].tags" -o json | jq '[.[] | select(. != null)]'
-o table (for lists) or -o yaml (for single resources)
Assigning one value to a shell variable
Assigning multiple values to shell variables
Iterating over a list of names/IDs
Writing output to a file for later use

JMESPath is a full query language for JSON. Beyond basic field selection, it supports pipes, flattening, multi-select objects, and built-in functions that cover most scripting needs without leaving the CLI.
When working with nested arrays—such as a VM that has multiple network interface cards, each with multiple IP configurations—the flatten operator [] unwraps one level of array nesting:

# List all private IP addresses across all NICs on a VM
az vm show -g myRG -n myVM \
  --query "networkProfile.networkInterfaces[].id" -o tsv

# Flatten two levels: NIC -> ipConfigurations -> privateIpAddress
az network nic list -g myRG \
  --query "[].ipConfigurations[].privateIpAddress" -o tsv

# Two-level flatten ([][] notation)
az network nic list -g myRG \
  --query "[*].ipConfigurations[][].{nic:name,ip:privateIpAddress}" -o table
The pipe operator | passes the current result to the next expression. @ refers to the current node—useful when calling functions on the full array:

# Count running VMs across a subscription
az vm list --query "[?powerState=='VM running'] | length(@)" -o tsv

# Reverse a sorted list
az vm list \
  --query "sort_by(@,&name) | reverse(@) | [*].{name:name,rg:resourceGroup}" \
  -o table

# First item of a filtered list
az storage account list \
  --query "[?sku.name=='Standard_LRS'] | [0].{name:name,location:location}" \
  -o json

# Find the newest resource group by creation time
az group list \
  --query "max_by(@,&properties.provisionedTime).{name:name,time:properties.provisionedTime}"

# Join all VM names into a comma-separated string
az vm list -g myRG --query "join(',', [*].name)" -o tsv

# Get all tag keys defined on a storage account
az storage account show -g myRG -n mysa \
  --query "keys(tags)" -o tsv

TSV (tab-separated values) produces the cleanest output for shell pipelines because it eliminates JSON delimiters. Fields with spaces are not quoted, which means field count is stable as long as values don't contain tabs.

# List all VM names (single column TSV = one name per line)
az vm list -g myRG --query "[*].name" -o tsv

# Two-column TSV: name and power state — pipe into while read
az vm list -g myRG \
  --query "[*].[name,powerState]" -o tsv |
while IFS=$'\t' read -r name state; do
  echo "VM: $name  State: $state"
done

# Feed into awk for conditional processing
az vm list -g myRG \
  --query "[*].[name,hardwareProfile.vmSize]" -o tsv |
awk -F'\t' '$2 ~ /Standard_D[0-9]+s/ { print "Resize candidate:", $1 }'

# Store as shell arrays
mapfile -t vm_names < <(az vm list -g myRG --query "[*].name" -o tsv)
echo "Total VMs: ${#vm_names[@]}"
for vm in "${vm_names[@]}"; do echo "  $vm"; done

# Stop all running VMs from a TSV list
az vm list -g myRG \
  --query "[?powerState=='VM running'].name" -o tsv |
xargs -P4 -I{} az vm stop -g myRG -n {} --no-wait

# Delete all empty resource groups (batch confirm required in production)
az group list --query "[?properties.provisioningState=='Succeeded'].name" -o tsv |
while read -r rg; do
  count=$(az resource list -g "$rg" --query "length(@)" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  if [[ "$count" == "0" ]]; then
    echo "Empty group: $rg"
  fi
done

While Azure CLI's built-in --query handles most tasks, jq offers richer stream transformations, regex support, and recursive descent. A common pattern is to use --query for initial server-side projection (reducing payload size) and jq for local reshaping:

# --query reduces the JSON; jq reshapes and adds computed fields
az vm list \
  --query "[*].{name:name,size:hardwareProfile.vmSize,location:location}" -o json |
jq '.[] | select(.location == "eastus") | {name, size, region: .location}'

# Recursive descent: find any key named "id" anywhere in the output
az resource show -g myRG -n myResource --resource-type Microsoft.Web/sites -o json |
jq '.. | .id? // empty'

# jq regex: VMs whose names start with "prod"
az vm list -o json | jq '[.[] | select(.name | test("^prod"))] | length'

# Group VMs by size using jq group_by
az vm list -o json |
jq 'group_by(.hardwareProfile.vmSize) |
    map({size: .[0].hardwareProfile.vmSize, count: length}) |
    sort_by(.count) | reverse'
Use --query when filtering or projecting fields and the data is simple. Switch to jq when you need regex, recursive descent, arithmetic, or custom transformations that JMESPath cannot express.

Automation contexts—GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, Jenkins, and scheduled scripts—each have different requirements for how CLI output should be consumed. Choosing the right format avoids brittle text parsing.

- name: Get App Service hostname
  run: |
    # Capture a single value as a step output
    HOSTNAME=$(az webapp show \
      --name my-app --resource-group myRG \
      --query defaultHostName -o tsv)
    echo "hostname=$HOSTNAME" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

- name: Matrix build - list all function apps
  id: list-apps
  run: |
    APPS=$(az functionapp list -g myRG \
      --query "[*].name" -o json)
    echo "apps=$APPS" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

- name: Deploy each app
  strategy:
    matrix:
      app: ${{ fromJson(steps.list-apps.outputs.apps) }}
  run: az functionapp deploy --name ${{ matrix.app }} ...

# Set a pipeline variable from CLI output
- script: |
    ACR_LOGIN=$(az acr show -n myacr --query loginServer -o tsv)
    echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=ACR_LOGIN]$ACR_LOGIN"
  displayName: Get ACR login server

- script: |
    # Parse JSON output for multi-value scenarios
    RESULT=$(az webapp list -g myRG -o json)
    COUNT=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq 'length')
    echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=APP_COUNT]$COUNT"
  displayName: Count web apps

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Emit machine-readable audit records during deployment
set -euo pipefail

deploy_and_log() {
  local rg=$1 name=$2
  result=$(az webapp deploy --name "$name" --resource-group "$rg" \
    --src-path app.zip --type zip -o json 2>&1)
  status=$(echo "$result" | jq -r '.provisioningState // "unknown"')
  printf '{"ts":"%s","name":"%s","status":"%s"}\n' \
    "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" "$name" "$status"
}

deploy_and_log myRG myapp | tee -a deploy-audit.jsonl

The -o flag and --query flag together control what data the CLI returns and in what shape. Use '-o table' for terminal inspection, '-o tsv' for shell scripting (variable assignment and loops), and '-o json' when passing results to other tools. The --query flag accepts JMESPath expressions: use '[].' to iterate arrays, '?condition' to filter, '{Key:field}' to project and rename fields, and 'length(@)' or 'sort_by(@, &field)' for aggregation. For more complex post-processing (grouping, joins), reduce the payload with --query first and then pipe to jq or Python.
